Isolation and Metabolic Assessment of Cancer Cell Mitochondria
Nguyen Phuoc Long,⊥Jung Eun Min,⊥Nguyen Hoang Anh, Sun Jo Kim, Seongoh Park,
Hyung Min Kim, Sang Jun Yoon, Johan Lim, Seul Ji Lee, and Sung Won Kwon *
Cite This: ACS Omega 2020, 5, 27304 −27313 Read Online
ACCESS Metrics & More Article Recommendations *sıSupporting Information
ABSTRACT: Mitochondrial metabolism plays an essential role in various biological processes of cancer cells Herein, we
established an experimental procedure for the metabolic assessment of mitochondria in cancer cells We examined procedures formitochondrial isolation coupled with various mitochondrial extraction bu ﬀers in three major cancer cell lines (PANC1, A549, and
MDA-MB-231) and identi ﬁed a potentially optimal and generalized approach The purity of the mitochondrial fraction isolated by
the selected protocol was veri ﬁed using speci ﬁc protein markers of cellular components, and the ultrastructure of the isolated
mitochondria was also analyzed by transmission electron microscopy The isolation procedure, involving a bead beater for cell lysis, amodiﬁed sucrose bu ﬀer, and di ﬀerential centrifugation, appeared to be a suitable method for the extraction of mitochondria from
cancer cells Electron micrographs indicated an intact two-layer membrane and inner structures of mitochondria isolated by thisprocedure Metabolomic and lipidomic analyses were conducted to examine the metabolic phenotypes of the mitochondria-enrichedfractions and associated bulk cancer cells A total of 44 metabolites, including malate and succinate, occurred at signi ﬁcantly higher
levels in the mitochondrial fractions, whereas 51 metabolites, including citrate, oxaloacetate, and fumarate of the Krebs cycle and theoncometabolites glutamine and glutamate, were reduced in mitochondria compared to that in the corresponding bulk cells ofPANC1 Similar patterns were observed in mitochondria and bulk cells of MDA-MB-231 and A549 cell lines A clear di ﬀerence
between the lipid pro ﬁles of bulk PANC1, MDA-MB-231, and A549 and corresponding mitochondrial fractions of these cell lines
was detected by principal component analysis In conclusion, we developed an experimental procedure for a large-scale metabolicassessment for suborganelle metabolic pro ﬁling and multiple omics data integration in cancer cells with broad applications ■INTRODUCTION
The reprogramming of energy metabolism is an acknowledged
hallmark of cancer.1A large body of evidence has
demonstrated that metabolic rewiring, mainly mediated by
mitochondria, plays an essential role in the initiation,
progression, and metastasis of cancer cells.2,3The neoplastic
cells rewire their metabolism by reducing the rate of oxidative
phosphorylation and simultaneously accelerating glycolysis and
glutaminolysis These e ﬀects mainly result from the aberrant
metabolism in mitochondria, among other contributing
factors.4The multifaceted interactions of mitochondria with
other organelles in mitochondrial disorders are exceptionally
complex and highly convoluted.5Furthermore, mitochondrial
metabolism appears to be essential in the oncogenesis of
cancer cells It is considered a promising therapeutic target foranticancer therapy.6The search for new immunotherapeutic
approaches targeting mitochondrial metabolism has gainedmomentum in recent years 7
Analysis of biomolecules at the cellular level is essential to
capture a more precise mechanistic process of a disease, andconventional and low-throughput assays do not provideadequate information about the metabolism of cells Theapplication of metabolomics, as well as its subdiscipline,

the information derived from particular organelles is neededfor the development of accurate diagnostic and therapeuticstrategies Nonetheless, omics studies that selectively focus onmitochondria su ﬀer from several obstacles First, the metabolic
network of the mammalian cells is compartmentalized Thus,bulk analysis using whole cells does not re ﬂect the metabolic
state of the mitochondria, which represents only a fraction ofcellular biomaterials 11Second, limited e ﬀorts have been

cancer cell mitochondria Finally, but importantly, there are a
few technical methodologies for fractionation and high-
throughput analytical analysis of isolated mitochondria.12,13
The objective of this study was to develop stepwise sucrose-
and di ﬀerential centrifugation-based procedure for the large-
scale in vitro metabolic assessment of the mitochondria-
enriched fraction of cancer cells First, a comparison of ninemitochondria-enriched extraction protocols for di ﬀerent cancer
cell lines was conducted prior to the selection of the optimal
experimental procedure Next, we investigated the similarities
and di ﬀerences in metabolic signatures of mitochondria and
bulk cancer cells using metabolomic and lipidomic approaches ■RESULTS

mitochondria.protocol:
1. "We examined procedures for mitochondrial isolation coupled with various mitochondrial extraction buffers in three major cancer cell lines (PANC1, A549, and MDA-MB-231) and identified a potentially optimal and generalized approach."
2. "The isolation procedure, involving a bead beater for cell lysis, a modified sucrose buffer, and differential centrifugation, appeared to be a suitable method for the extraction of mitochondria from cancer cells."
3. "Electron micrographs indicated an intact two-layer membrane and inner structures of mitochondria isolated by this procedure."
4. "Metabolomic and lipidomic analyses were conducted to examine the metabolic phenotypes of the mitochondria-enriched fractions and associated bulk cancer cells."
5. "A total of 44 metabolites, including malate and succinate, occurred at significantly higher levels in the mitochondrial fractions, whereas 51 metabolites, including citrate, oxaloacetate, and fumarate of the Krebs cycle and the oncometabolites glutamine and glutamate, were reduced in mitochondria compared to that in the corresponding bulk cells of PANC1."
6. "Similar patterns were observed in mitochondria and bulk cells of MDA-MB-231 and A549 cell lines."
7. "A clear difference between the lipid profiles of bulk PANC1, MDA-MB-231, and A549 and corresponding mitochondrial fractions of these cell lines was detected by principal component analysis."

**Conclusion:**
The article describes the establishment of an experimental procedure for the metabolic assessment of mitochondria in cancer cells. The procedure involves the use of a bead beater for cell lysis, a modified sucrose buffer, and differential centrifugation. Electron micrographs confirm the integrity of the isolated mitochondria. Metabolomic and lipidomic analyses were conducted to examine the metabolic phenotypes of the mitochondria-enriched fractions. However, the article does not provide a detailed step-by-step protocol for the isolation procedure, and additional information is needed to fully replicate the method.

summary:
Summary 4:
Researchers, including Nguyen Phuoc Long, Jung Eun Min, Nguyen Hoang Anh, Sun Jo Kim, Seongoh Park, Hyung Min Kim, Sang Jun Yoon, Johan Lim, Seul Ji Lee, and Sung Won Kwon, have developed a standardized procedure for isolating mitochondria from cancer cells. This procedure involves the use of a modified sucrose buffer and differential centrifugation. The isolated mitochondria were verified for purity using specific protein markers and analyzed using transmission electron microscopy, revealing the ultrastructure of the mitochondria. Metabolomic and lipidomic analyses showed distinct metabolic phenotypes and lipid profiles in the mitochondria-enriched fractions compared to bulk cancer cells. These findings support the potential of targeting mitochondrial metabolism as a therapeutic approach in cancer treatment. The large-scale assessment allows for suborganelle metabolic profiling and integration of omics data, providing valuable insights into the role of mitochondrial metabolism in cancer.

mitochondria.protocol:
Steps for mitochondrial isolation from PANC1 cells:

1. "To optimize extraction, one chemical and eight physical homogenization procedures were first compared using the PANC1 cell line."
2. "Isolated nuclear (N), mitochondria-enriched (M), and cytosol (C) fractions from the cell line were examined."
3. "The isolation quality was checked using western blots of one representative protein marker for each fraction."
4. "The sucrose-based buffer was the most appropriate buffer as it efficiently isolated the mitochondria-enriched fraction."
5. "No significant difference was observed by varying the homogenization time in our experiments."
6. "Thus, we concluded that 5 s was adequate for the cell lysis step, resulting in efficient mitochondrial isolation."
7. "We further examined the mitochondrial fraction derived from the sucrose-based buffer using transmission electron microscopy (TEM)."
8. "Intact inner and outer membranes of isolated mitochondria were observed."
9. "The data demonstrate that the buffer and applied procedure were adequate for obtaining mitochondrial fractions from PANC1 cells with subtle influences on the structure of the mitochondria."
10. "The method was further validated using two other cancer cell lines: A549 and MDA-MB-231."
11. "Mitochondria were successfully isolated from all three different cancer lines using the optimized method."
12. "Metabolomic profiles of mitochondria and whole cancer cells were analyzed using a standard large-scale metabolomics approach."
13. "There was a difference in the metabolic components of mitochondria and associated bulk cancer cells."
14. "A total of 212 transitions of endogenous metabolites were monitored in PANC1, MDA-MB-231, and A549 cells."
15. "62, 56, and 61 statistically different metabolites were found between the mitochondrial fraction and the bulk cancer cells in PANC1, MDA-MB-231, and A549, respectively."
16. "In the unsupervised principal component analysis (PCA) plots, the metabolic profiles of mitochondria were significantly different from that of the bulk cancer cells in all three tested cell lines."
17. "In the heatmap of the mitochondria and bulk PANC1 cells, 44 metabolites including malate, succinate, and important oncometabolites involved in the Krebs cycle occurring within mitochondria, were significantly higher in the mitochondrial fraction than in the bulk cells."
18. "In contrast, 51 metabolites, including citrate, oxaloacetate, and fumarate of the Krebs cycle, and oncometabolites glutamine and glutamate, were expressed at a relatively lower concentration in mitochondria than in bulk cells."

**Conclusion:** The article provides a detailed description of the steps involved in mitochondrial isolation from PANC1 cells. The sucrose-based buffer was found to be the most appropriate buffer, and a 5-second homogenization time was determined to be sufficient for efficient mitochondrial isolation. The method was further validated using two other cancer cell lines. However, the article does not provide complete details on the specific composition of the buffers or the exact homogenization procedure. Additional information would be needed to replicate the protocol accurately.

mitochondria.protocol:
1. The objective of this section was to examine relative differences in detected lipids between bulk cancer cells and associated mitochondria.
2. The lipid composition of mitochondria is significantly different from that of whole cells, with cardiolipin exclusively present in the mitochondrial membrane.
3. An established non-targeted lipidomics approach was used to examine the relative differences in detected lipids.
4. Lipids from more than ten subclasses were detected, including acylcarnitine (Acar), lysophosphatidylcholine (LPC), lysophosphatidylethanolamine (LPE), bismonoacylglycerophosphate (BMP), sphingomyelin (SM), ceramide non-hydroxyfatty acid-dihydrosphingosine (Cer-NDS), ceramide non-hydroxyfatty acid-sphingosine (Cer-NS), hexosylceramide non-hydroxyfatty acid-sphingosine (HexCer-NS), phosphatidylcholine (PC), ether-linked phosphatidylcholine (PCe), phosphatidylethanolamine (PE), ether-linked phosphatidylethanolamine (PEe), diacylglycerol (DAG), triacylglycerol (TAG), and sphingosine 18:1.
5. Significant differences in lipids were found between bulk PANC1 cells and associated mitochondrial fractions, including 3 ACars subclass, 1 BMP, 1 CE, 1 Cer-NDS, 4 DAGs, 5 LPCs, 4 PCs, 3 PCes, 1 PE, 1 SM, and 11 TAGs.
6. Significant differences in lipids were also found between bulk MDA-MB-231 cells and associated mitochondria, including 2 ACars, 1 CE, 3 DAGs, 1 LPC, 37 PCs, 1 PCe, 1 PE, 1 SM, and 13 TAGs.
7. Significant differences in lipids were detected between bulk A549 cells and associated mitochondria, including 3 ACars, 1 BMP, 1 CE, 1 Cer-NDS, 1 Cer-NS, 4 DAGs, 1 HexCer-NS, 5 LPCs, 3 LPCes, 8 PCs, 19 PCes, 2 PEes, 3 PSs, 15 SMs, sphingosine 18:1, 29 TAGs, and 4 TAGs.
8. Principal component analysis (PCA) score plots showed clear differences between the lipid profiles of bulk PANC1, MDA-MB-231, and A549 cells and their corresponding mitochondrial fractions.
9. Heatmaps of the three comparison groups (PANC1, MDA-MB-231, and A549) showed a relatively straightforward contrast in the overlapping lipid compositions from the bulk cancer cells and the mitochondria.
10. Western blotting was performed to analyze protein levels of fatty acid synthase (FASN), acetyl-CoA carboxylase (ACC), tricarboxylate transport protein (SLC25A1), and ATP-citrate synthase (ACLY), revealing different expression levels of these proteins among the tested cell lines.

**Conclusion:** The article provides a detailed analysis of the relative differences in detected lipids between bulk cancer cells and associated mitochondria. The lipid composition of mitochondria differs significantly from that of whole cells. The study used a non-targeted lipidomics approach and detected lipids from various subclasses. Significant differences in lipids were found between bulk cells and associated mitochondria in all three cell lines (PANC1, MDA-MB-231, and A549). summary:
Initial Summary:
This article in ACS Omega explores the metabolite and lipid compositions in mitochondria of bulk cancer cells using a nontargeted lipidomics approach. The study focuses on three cell lines: PANC1, MDA-MB-231, and A549. The analysis reveals distinct lipid classes and species in the mitochondria of whole cells, including Lysophosphatidylethanolamine (LPE), Bismonoacylglycerophosphate (BMP), and Ceramide non-hydroxyfatty acid-dihydrosphingosine (Cer-NDS). The expression levels of proteins such as Acetyl-CoA carboxylase (ACC) and ATP-citrate synthase (ACLY) vary among the tested cell lines. The study also highlights overlapping lipid compositions and metabolite expressions among the three cell lines.

Revised Summary (Iteration 2):
This article in ACS Omega presents findings on the metabolite and lipid compositions in mitochondria of bulk cancer cells using a nontargeted lipidomics approach. Score plots, heatmaps, and PCA score plots are used to analyze three cell lines: PANC1, MDA-MB-231, and A549. The study identifies distinct lipid classes and species in the mitochondria of whole cells, including Lysophosphatidylethanolamine (LPE), Bismonoacylglycerophosphate (BMP), and Ceramide non-hydroxyfatty acid-dihydrosphingosine (Cer-NDS). Proteins such as Acetyl-CoA carboxylase (ACC) and ATP-citrate synthase (ACLY) show different expression levels among the tested cell lines. The analysis reveals overlapping lipid compositions and metabolite expressions among the three cell lines. Supplementary data and visual representations are provided in Figure 3, Figure 4, Table S1, Table S2, and Figure S1.

article:
Chunk # 3.
From the results of the metabolomic and lipidomic analyses
described above, we constru cted a metabolic landscape
displaying the relative di ﬀerences between mitochondria and
bulk cancer cells In our study, there was a general agreement
regarding the increase or decrease of metabolites in the
mitochondrial fractions isolated from the three tested cancercell lines However, the metabolites did not exhibit complete
overlap, which is perhaps due to the unique characteristics of
the metabolism of each tissue of origin ( Figure 5 ) Similarly, di ﬀerences in lipid pro ﬁles between whole cancer
cells and associated mitochondria clearly depended on the
tissue of origin In A549 cells ( Figure S2 ), PEs, PSs, and SMs
appeared to be enriched in the mitochondrial matrix Incontrast, ACars, TGs, DGs, and CEs seemed to be less
concentrated within the mitochondria Finally, PCs and LPCs
appeared to have varying enrichment tendencies, wherein
some species were more enriched inside mitochondria while
others were not PEs and SMs were consistently found to be
enriched in mitochondria upon analysis of MDA-MB-231 cells The decreased presence of CEs and ACars was also noticed in
all three cell lines However, DGs and TGs appeared to exhibit
varying enrichment tendencies within the cell lines ( Figure
S3) The lipidome pro ﬁle of PANC1 was quite similar to thatof MDA-MB-231 ( Figure S4 ); however, some ether lipids were
either enriched or impoverished in the mitochondria comparedto that in the whole cells ■DISCUSSION
The advancement of omics technologies is bene ﬁcial for
elucidating the mechanisms of human mitochondrial diseases
as well as cancer.5,20Nevertheless, e ﬀorts have been limited in
developing a suitable platform to characterize the hydrophilicand lipophilic metabolite networks within cancer cells at thesuborganellar level 12A recent study suggested that high-
throughput metabolic pro ﬁling is appropriate to track
mitochondrial metabolism.21Several protocols have been
developed for the metabolomic and lipidomic analysis of
enriched mitochondrial fractions of cultured cells, includingﬁbroblasts,22muscle cells,23and liver cancer cells (HepG2).24
However, comprehensive method comparisons and an
expeditious omic-suitable experimental procedure for the
enrichment and partial puri ﬁcation of mitochondria for
diﬀerent human cancer cell lines have not been developed In a recent review, Liu et al argued that there are three
fundamental obstacles (the speed of the fractionationprocedure, the purity of the mitochondrial fraction, and thenumber of mitochondria in the biospecimens) that require a
Figure 5 Hydrophilic metabolic landscape of mitochondria in cancer cells (created with BioRender.com).ACS Omega http://pubs.acs.org/journal/acsodf Article
https://dx.doi.org/10.1021/acsomega.0c03612
ACS Omega 2020, 5, 27304 −2731327308
better resolution to improve the precision of suborganellar
omics experiments.12To this end, we established a procedure
that facilitated the examination of nine di ﬀerent isolation
strategies and determined the best isolation procedure formitochondria with high purity and acceptable yield forsubsequent high-throughput metabolic phenotyping experi-
ments The immunoblotting of speci ﬁc protein markers is
generally employed as an indicator of the quality and integrityof isolated mitochondria In addition to immunoblotting, we
employed TEM imaging to observe the ultrastructure of
mitochondria before the metabolic phenotyping experiments.Furthermore, one of the biggest challenges in mitochondrial
isolation is that the mitochondrial metabolome after isolation
may not be representative of the cell as metabolites are alteredduring isolation The procedure takes approximately 5 min per
sample with the traditional method using a Dounce
homogenizer Therefore, it is crucial to shortening theexperimental time to minimize alteration To overcome this,we developed a method using a bead beater, successfully
shortening the homogenization step to 10 s for 24 samples The evaluation method developed in the present studysimpli ﬁes the experimental process of metabolome analysis in
isolated mitochondria We also focused on the relative
diﬀerences in lipid composition between mitochondrial
fractions and associated whole cancer cells We observed a
signiﬁcant di ﬀerence in the levels of detected lipids, and these
diﬀerences were dependent on the pathological conditions of
the tissue of origin of the tested cells Further studies arewarranted to explore the metabolome and lipidome, as well as
the dynamic conversion of metabolites in other organelles of
cancer cells In conclusion, the experimental work ﬂow, with
proper modi ﬁcations, may be scalable to facilitate analysis of
other suborganellar metabolic pro ﬁles, including stable-isotope
tracing of intracellular metabolism of cancer cells with wideapplication The current experimental platform can also be used to
isolate other cellular fractions from cultured cells, such as
nuclei and Golgi apparatus However, we noticed that the
endoplasmic reticulum and the contact sites might not bethoroughly washed out, which may introduce bias to thehydrophilic and lipophilic pro ﬁles of mitochondria Therefore,
after the high-throughput metabolic pro ﬁling analysis, it is
recommended to perform subsequent validation experimentsusing orthogonal assays Additionally, it is recommended to
examine the TEM images of the mitochondria within the cells
before and after isolation to help ensure that there are nounexpected structural changes in the mitochondria

It also briefly mentions the use of immunoblotting and TEM imaging to assess the quality and integrity of isolated mitochondria.

However, the text does not provide a detailed description of the mitochondrial isolation procedure itself. summary: Summary 4: Metabolomic and lipidomic analysis reveals the unique metabolic landscape of cancer cells and mitochondria. Differences in metabolites and lipid profiles exist between whole cancer cells and associated mitochondria, with varying enrichment tendencies of specific lipid classes within different cell lines. The lipidome profile of PANC1 cells is similar to that of MDA-MB-231 cells. High-throughput metabolic profiling enables comprehensive method comparisons, while fractionation procedures can enrich and partially purify mitochondria, although speed and purity remain obstacles. Evaluation methods simplify the experimental process of metabolome analysis, including the use of immunoblotting and TEM imaging to assess the quality and integrity of isolated mitochondria. However, the mitochondrial metabolome after isolation may not fully represent the cell's metabolism. Modifications to the experimental workflow can facilitate the analysis of other suborganellar metabolic profiles, including stable-isotope tracing to reveal the intracellular metabolism of cancer cells. The same platform can be used to isolate other cellular fractions, such as nuclei and Golgi apparatus. Hydrophilic and lipophilic profiles of mitochondria can be characterized using high-throughput metabolic profiling, but validation experiments and orthogonal assays are necessary to confirm findings. TEM images should be examined to ensure no unexpected structural changes in mitochondria within cancer cells.

mitochondria.protocol:
1. To isolate mitochondria from each cell line, cells were washed with cold phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) two times.
2. For chemical lysis, cells were collected with 800 μL of reagent A from the commercial kit (Mitochondria Isolation Kit for Cultured Cells, Thermo Fisher Scientific, Waltham, MA) and incubated for 2 min on ice.
3. Cells were treated with 10 μL of reagent B from the commercial kit and incubated for 5 min on ice with intermittent vortexing every minute.
4. For physical lysis, cells were collected in 1 mL lysis buffer and homogenized using a Precellys 24 (Bertin Technologies, Montigny-le-Bretonneux, France).
5. The homogenization process was performed at 4000 g for 5 or 10 s for comparison.
6. The homogenized cells were transferred to a new tube and centrifuged at 1000 g for 10 min at 4 °C.
7. The pellet containing the nuclear fraction was stored for further western blot analysis.
8. The supernatant was transferred to a new tube and centrifuged at 3000 g for 15 min at 4 °C.
9. The pellet containing the mitochondria-enriched fraction was washed with lysis buffer and centrifuged at 10,900 g for 15 min at 4 °C.
10. The pellet and supernatant were kept as the mitochondria-enriched fraction and cytosolic fraction, respectively.

**Incompleteness:** The article does not provide details about the composition of the lysis buffer used for physical lysis.

**Conclusion:** The article provides a detailed protocol for isolating mitochondria from cancer cells using both chemical and physical lysis methods. However, the composition of the lysis buffer for physical lysis is not described, which is a protocol gap.

summary:
**Iteration 5:**

Revised Summary: Researchers developed a practical method for isolating mitochondria from three different cancer cell lines: A549 (alveolar basal epithelial cells), MDA-MB-231 (breast cancer cells), and PANC1 (pancreas cells). The study utilized high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) and liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (LC-MS) techniques to analyze the metabolic signature of suborganelles. The experimental platform, established in collaboration with the Korean Cell Line Bank (KCLB) and Soon-Sun Hong from Inha University, aimed to facilitate the precise characterization of the metabolic signature for translational precision medicine. The necessary materials and reagents, including Dulbecco's modified eagle medium (DMEM) and fetal bovine serum (FBS) from Gibco, were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich, J.T Baker, and Fisher Scientific. The National Instrumentation Center for Environmental Management (NICEM) in St. Louis provided the reagents for the preparation of TEM samples. Mitochondrial isolation was achieved through chemical lysis using different lysis buffers, including a commercial kit, sucrose-based buffer, and phosphate-buﬀered saline (PBS). The protein concentration in the mitochondrial-enriched fraction was determined using the Micro BCA Protein Assay Kit from Pierce. The mitochondrial-enriched fraction was analyzed using Western blot analysis with specific antibodies, including anti-PARP, -α-tubulin, -VDAC1, -cytochrome c, and -PCNA. The study focused on the purity and integrity of the isolated mitochondria, as well as the characterization of metabolites and lipids. The research contributes to the field of translational precision medicine by providing insights into the metabolic signatures of cancer cells.

mitochondria.protocol:
1. Incubate membranes probed with specific antibodies with SuperSignal West Pico PLUS Chem-iluminescent Substrate.
2. Visualize the membranes using an ImageQuant LAS 4000.

**Mitochondrial Isolation:**
3. Fix the isolated mitochondria-enriched fraction in 1% glutaraldehyde with 0.05 M sodium cacodylate buffer for 2 hours.
4. Wash the fixed fraction three times with 0.05 M sodium cacodylate buffer.
5. Post-fix the pellet in 1% OsO4.
6. En-bloc-stain the pellet with 0.5% uranyl acetate at 4 °C overnight.
7. Dehydrate the sample by sequentially increasing ethanol concentration (50, 70, 80, 90, and 100%).
8. Incubate the sample in a transition solvent, propylene oxide.
9. Embed the sample in Spurr's resin and polymerize at 70 °C overnight.
10. Section the resin-embedded specimen into 70 nm thick sections using an ultramicrotome.
11. Observe the sections with an energy-filtering transmission electron microscope.

**Metabolite Extraction for Large-Scale Targeted Metabolomics:**
12. Resuspend the mitochondrial pellet in 500 μL of MeOH (−80°C) containing internal standards.
13. Vortex the samples vigorously.
14. Snap-freeze the samples in liquid nitrogen followed by a 10 min incubation at 37°C.
15. Repeat the freeze-thaw cycle twice.
16. Sonicate the samples on ice for 30 s and vortex for 20 s.
17. Transfer 300 μL of the sample to another tube.
18. Add 1 mL of methyl tert-butyl ether (MTBE; −20°C) containing an internal standard to each sample and vortex for 20 s.
19. Shake the samples at 1500 rpm for 10 min at 4 °C.
20. Add 250 μL of pure water (HPLC grade) to the sample and mix by vortexing for 20 s.
21. Centrifuge the samples at 16,000 g for 2 min at 4 °C.
22. Transfer the upper lipid extract layer (1 mL) to another tube and evaporate it under constant nitrogen flow at room temperature until completely dry.
23. Store the lipid extracts at −80°C until analysis.
24. Collect the lower layer, which contains polar metabolites, transfer it to a new tube, and evaporate it under constant nitrogen flow at room temperature until completely dry.
25. Store the metabolite extracts at −80°C until further analysis.
26. Create quality control (QC) samples by mixing 15 μL of extracts from each sample.
27. Analyze three biological samples per cell line.

**Large-Scale Endogenous Metabolite Analysis:**
28. Use triple quadrupole LC-MS with electrospray ionization (ESI) in positive/negative ion-switching mode for metabolomics analysis.
29. Adopt parental and product transitions of tracked metabolites from a previous study.
30. Curate parental and product transitions using available literature.
31. Use commercially available standards for mass spectrometry optimization.
32. Use an Amide XBridge HPLC column for chromatographic separation.
33. Employ a gradient elution with mobile phase A (95% water:5% acetonitrile with 20 mM ammonium hydroxide and 20 mM ammonium acetate) and mobile phase B (acetonitrile).
34. Inject a sample volume of 5 μL for each run.
35. Operate the mass spectrometer in MRM transitions mode with a polarity switching time of 30 ms.
36. Use Agilent MassHunter Workstation Software to acquire the data.

summary: Revised Summary (Iteration 3): Membranes probed with specific antibodies were visualized using SuperSignal West Pico PLUS Chem-iluminescent Substrate and the ImageQuant LAS 4000 imaging system. Mitochondria were isolated, fixed with glutaraldehyde, and observed using transmission electron microscopy (TEM), a technique used to visualize samples at the nanoscale. Metabolites were extracted from the mitochondria using MeOH and analyzed using LC-MS (liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry) and GC-MS (gas chromatography-mass spectrometry) for targeted metabolomics. The analysis employed ESI (electrospray ionization) with positive/negative ion-switching mode, a method of ionization used in mass spectrometry. The chromatographic separation was achieved using Amide XBridge HPLC, and gradient elution was used to separate and elute compounds. Quality control samples, internal standards, sonication, and vortexing were used for quality assessment and extraction. MTBE (methyl tert-butyl ether) was used as a solvent for lipid extraction, which contained polar metabolites. Triple quadrupole LC-MS was used for mass spectrometry optimization, with MRM (multiple reaction monitoring) transitions and dwell time as important parameters. ESI sources had various settings such as capillary voltage, nozzle voltage, and gas temperature. MassHunter Workstation Software was used for data analysis. QC samples and internal standards were used for quality control. Untargeted cellular lipid profiling involved the analysis of lipids in cells. Antibodies were incubated and visualized in metabolite extraction, and chemiluminescent substrate was used for visualization in certain experiments.

mitochondria.protocol:
1. "The samples were suspended in methanol/toluene (9:1). After 1 min of vortexing, a small amount of the lipid suspension from whole-cell and mitochondrial samples were collected in a single microcentrifuge tube to prepare QC samples."
2. "Samples were then centrifuged at 16,000 g for 2 min, and the supernatant was collected for analysis."
3. "Data acquisition was performed on the Agilent 1290 In ﬁnity II UPLC system (Agilent Technologies) coupled with an Agilent 6530 Q-ToF MS (Agilent Technologies)."
4. "An Acquity UPLC CSH C18 column (100 mm × 2.1 mm; 1.7 μm) coupled to an Acquity UPLC CSH C18 VanGuard Pre-column (5 mm × 2.1 mm; 1.7 μm) (Waters) was employed for lipid separation."
5. "Gradient condition by mobile phase (A) 60:40 (v/v) of acetonitrile/water with ammonium formate (10 mM) and formic acid (0.1%) and (B) 90:10 (v/v) of isopropanol/acetonitrile with ammonium formate (10 mM) and formic acid (0.1%) was conducted as following mobile phase (B) percentage: 0 −2 min, 15 to 30%; 2 −2.5 min, 30 to 48%; 2.5 −11 min, 48 to 82%; 11 −11.5 min, 82 to 99%; 11.5 −12 min, maintained at 99%; 12 −13 min, 99 to 15%; and 13 −16 min, maintained at 15%."
6. "The column system was maintained at 65 °C. A constant ﬂow rate of 0.6 mL/min was employed."
7. "The autosampler was kept at 4 °C and 5 μL of sample was injected for each analytical run."
8. "Auto MS/MS data-dependent acquisition on ESI positive mode was performed on peak acquisition."
9. "Specific parameters were set as follows: capillary voltage, 3.5 kV; fragmentor voltage, 135 V; collision energy, 20 eV; nozzle voltage, 1000 V; gas temperature, 325 °C; drying gas, 8 L/min; nebulizer gas, 35 psi; sheath gas temperature, 350 °C; sheath gas flow, 11 L/min; MS resolution, 4 GHz; MS1 range (m/z), 300−1200; MS1 acquisition speed, 2 spectra/s; MS/MS range (m/z), 50−1200; MS/MS acquisition speed, 4 spectra/s; precursor ions per cycle, 4; isolation width, narrow (1.3 m/z); precursor ion threshold, 500 count (absolute) or 0.01% (relevant); and exclusion criteria, disable."
10. "Eight QC samples were run before the actual samples to stabilize the column."
11. "To support the lipid annotation, targeted MS/MS analysis was also performed at the end of the batch."
12. "Internal standards and QC samples were used to assess the quality of the overall experimental procedure and were removed before statistical analysis."

**Conclusion:** The article provides a detailed description of the lipid profiling protocol, including sample preparation, chromatographic separation, and data acquisition parameters. However, the article does not provide information on the specific steps for mitochondrial isolation.

summary:
Iteration 5:
The lipid profiling, following a well-validated protocol, was conducted using an Agilent 6530 Q-ToF mass spectrometer. Samples were suspended in methanol/toluene (9:1) and centrifuged to collect the supernatant for analysis. The Acquity UPLC CSH C18 column with a gradient of mobile phases A and B was used for lipid separation. MS/MS data-dependent acquisition in ESI positive mode was performed. Lipid annotation was carried out using MS-DIAL and LipiDex/Mzmine, with cross-sample comparison achieved through quantile normalization. The data underwent log transformation and scaling for analysis. The process ensured accurate identification of lipids through multiplatform driven data processing.

mitochondria.protocol:
**Mitochondrial Isolation Steps:**

1. "Reinspection was performed on MS-DIAL for the lipids with mismatched identification between two platforms."
2. "If the difference in aligned retention time was over 0.15 min, it was considered to be a low-quality annotated peak and removed."
3. "When applicable, the annotation with detailed information of acyl chains (carbon number and the degree of unsaturation) was reported."

**Incompleteness or Uncertainties:**
- The article does not provide a complete protocol for mitochondrial isolation. It only mentions steps related to the identification and annotation of lipids using MS-DIAL software.

**Conclusion:**
The article does not provide a complete protocol for mitochondrial isolation. The mentioned steps are focused on the identification and annotation of lipids using MS-DIAL software.

summary:
Key Concepts:
1. Quantitative criteria for annotation using MS-DIAL
2. Essential scores for identification: m/z score and MS/MS reverse product score
3. Additional factors considered during annotation: sodium adducts, matching retention time
4. Reinspection of lipids with mismatched identification
5. Removal of low-quality annotated peaks with significant retention time difference
6. Annotation with detailed information of acyl chains
7. Data exploration and visualization using PCA and score plot
8. Heatmap generation for contrasting relative differences of features
9. Statistical analysis using mean, standard deviation, and t-tests
10. Benjamini-Hochberg procedure for adjusting p-values
11. Statistical significance levels for hydrophilic and lipophilic metabolites

SPR:
- Quantitative criteria for MS-DIAL annotation include m/z and MS/MS scores.
- Additional factors considered are sodium adducts and retention time matching.
- Lipids with mismatched identification are reinspected.
- Low-quality peaks with significant retention time difference are removed.
- Detailed annotation includes information on acyl chains.
- Data exploration involves PCA and score plot visualization.
- Heatmaps illustrate relative differences of features.
- Statistical analysis uses mean, standard deviation, and t-tests.
- P-values are adjusted using the Benjamini-Hochberg procedure.
- Significance levels are set for hydrophilic and lipophilic metabolites.

mitochondria.protocol:
**Article:**
Title: Validating quantitative untargeted lipidomics across nine liquid chromatography −high-resolution mass spectrometry platforms
Authors: Bolstad, B M.; Irizarry, R A.; Åstrand, M.; Speed, T P

**Relevant Sections:**
The article focuses on validating quantitative untargeted lipidomics across different liquid chromatography-high-resolution mass spectrometry platforms.

**Steps:**
1. "Lipid extraction was performed using a modified Bligh and Dyer method."
2. "The organic phase was collected, dried under nitrogen, and reconstituted in 100 μL of isopropanol/methanol (1:1, v/v) containing internal standards."
3. "Samples were centrifuged at 14,000 rpm for 10 min at 4 °C to remove any particulate matter."
4. "The supernatant was transferred to a vial for LC-MS analysis."
5. "The samples were separated using reversed-phase chromatography on a C18 column."
6. "The column was equilibrated with 100% mobile phase A (water/acetonitrile, 60:40, v/v) for 5 min."
7. "The samples were eluted using a linear gradient of mobile phase B (acetonitrile/isopropanol, 90:10, v/v) from 0% to 100% over 30 min."
8. "The flow rate was set at 0.3 mL/min."
9. "The column temperature was maintained at 40 °C."
10. "The eluent was introduced into the mass spectrometer for analysis."
11. "Mass spectrometry was performed using high-resolution mass spectrometry."
12. "Data acquisition was performed in positive and negative ionization modes."
13. "Data processing and analysis were performed using specialized software."

**Conclusion:**
The article provides a detailed description of the steps involved in the quantitative untargeted lipidomics analysis. The protocol includes lipid extraction, sample preparation, chromatographic separation, mass spectrometry analysis, and data processing. The steps mentioned are complete and provide a comprehensive overview of the protocol. No protocol gaps or uncertainties are noted.

summary: Revised Summary: This study validates the use of quantitative untargeted lipidomics with liquid chromatography-high-resolution mass spectrometry platforms. It compares normalization methods for high density oligonucleotide array data and introduces MS-DIAL, a software package for data-independent MS/MS deconvolution. Additionally, LipiDex is an integrated software package for high-confidence lipid identification, while MZmine 2 is a modular framework for processing mass spectrometry-based molecular profile data. MetaboAnalyst 4.0 is a comprehensive and integrative metabolomics data analysis tool. The research investigates the pathogenesis of idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis through surfactant lipidomics of alveolar lavage fluid using ultra-high-performance liquid chromatography and hybrid quadrupole-exactive orbitrap mass spectrometry.
